title: TabArena: A Living Benchmark for Machine Learning on Tabular Data
authors: Nick Erickson (AWS), Lennart Purucker (U. Freiburg), Andrej Tschalzev (U. Mannheim), David Holzmüller (INRIA/ENS/PSL), Prateek Mutalik Desai (AWS), David Salinas (ELLIS Tübingen/Freiburg), Frank Hutter (Prior Labs/ELLIS/Freiburg)
venue: arXiv:2506.16791 (v4, Nov 2025); NeurIPS 2025 Datasets & Benchmarks Track (spotlight)
tokenization_method: None of its own — TabArena is a benchmarking SYSTEM, not a tokenizer. Its relevance is that it is the most carefully-curated head-to-head evaluation harness for the deep-tabular and foundation models whose tokenizers this topic studies. It standardizes preprocessing (model-agnostic + per-model preprocessing inside AutoGluon's AbstractModel framework), HPO (1 default + 200 random configs, 8-fold inner CV for config selection), and post-hoc weighted ensembling, then ranks 16 models by Elo. So it indirectly answers: under fair, tuned, ensembled evaluation on real IID tables, which tokenization/embedding regimes (per-feature transformer-style tokenizers in RealMLP/TabM, per-cell prior-fitted tokenizers in TabPFNv2/TabICL/TabDPT, vs GBDT split-based feature use) actually win, and on what dataset sizes.
feature_types_handled: numerical, categorical (including measured per-dataset categorical fraction 0-100%), and missing values — mixed, real-world IID tables. Explicitly NO temporal / time-series / non-IID data in v0.1 (deliberately excluded during curation; left for future versions). So it covers the static, heterogeneous numerical+categorical part of this topic's setting but NOT the temporal-static-fusion part.
token_granularity: n/a for the benchmark itself; the models it ranks span per-feature tokens (FT-Transformer lineage, RealMLP/TabM embeddings), per-cell tokens (TabPFNv2/TabICL/TabDPT), and non-tokenized split features (GBDTs, EBM). TabArena is the arena where these granularities compete.
numerical_strategy: delegated to each model; TabArena fixes a common AutoGluon preprocessing layer plus model-specific preprocessing so that, e.g., RealMLP's robust scaling / numerical embeddings and GBDT raw-split handling each run as their authors intended. Not a numerical-embedding contribution.
categorical_strategy: delegated to each model and reported as a dataset characteristic (categorical-feature percentage per dataset, plotted in Fig. 3). No new categorical encoder; but the 51-dataset suite is a usable testbed for categorical/high-cardinality tokenizers because real categorical-heavy datasets are present.
high_cardinality_handling: not a method; the curated suite contains real-world tables so high-cardinality categoricals occur naturally, but the paper does not isolate high-cardinality performance. Foundation-model results are capped at <=500 features (a column count, not a cardinality limit), so high-cardinality stress-testing is not a reported axis.
temporal_handling: none — explicitly out of scope for v0.1. Datasets requiring temporal or group-based (non-random) splits were filtered out during curation; time-series-forecasting and non-IID data are deferred to future TabArena releases. This is the single biggest gap relative to this topic's temporal-static-fusion layer.
scales_to_many_features: Directly informative. Curated datasets span ~10 to ~1000 features (log-scale axis in Fig. 3) and 500 to 250,000 samples — squarely the 'small-to-medium' regime, overlapping this topic's 70+-feature target on the feature axis but capping samples at 250k. CRITICAL constraint: foundation-model tokenizers (TabPFNv2, TabICL) are evaluated ONLY on the <=500-feature, <=10k-sample (TabPFNv2) / <=100k-sample classification (TabICL) subsets — 33 and 36 datasets respectively. So the benchmark provides fair evidence that per-cell prior-fitted tokenizers win below ~500 features / small samples, but provides NO evidence they work above 500 features. Deep MLP/embedding models (RealMLP, TabM, ModernNCA) and GBDTs run on the full 51-dataset suite including the wider/larger tables.
downstream_model: 16 models: GBDTs (XGBoost, LightGBM, CatBoost), tree baselines (RandomForest, ExtraTrees), EBM, deep nets (FastaiMLP, TorchMLP, RealMLP, TabM-mini, ModernNCA), 3 foundation models (TabPFNv2, TabICL, TabDPT), and Linear/KNN baselines; plus AutoGluon 1.3 (4h, best_quality) as a reference pipeline. Implemented in AutoGluon's AbstractModel (sklearn-compatible) framework. ~25,000,000 model instances trained; ~15 wall-clock-years of compute.
pretraining_objective: n/a for the benchmark; among ranked models, TabPFNv2/TabICL/TabDPT carry their own in-context / prior-fitted pretraining objectives. Foundation models are refit on train+val (no CV-ensembling) per their authors' recommendations, whereas all other models use 8-fold CV ensembles.
key_results: (1) Post-hoc ensembling of tuned hyperparameter configs is what reveals peak performance: the top-3 single models on the full leaderboard (TabM, LightGBM, RealMLP) would all rank BELOW the 4th model (CatBoost) without post-hoc ensembling. In the conventional single-config tuned regime CatBoost ranks #1 (replicating prior work). (2) Under tuning+ensembling, the best deep nets (TabM, RealMLP) equal or beat the best GBDTs on the full 51-dataset IID suite. (3) Foundation models lead on small data: on the <=500-feature/<=10k-sample subset (33 datasets) TabPFNv2 outperforms all others by a large margin, and TabPFNv2 tuned+ensembled beats the AutoGluon 4h reference. (4) Cross-model ensembles advance SOTA, but some deep-learning models are overrepresented in those ensembles due to VALIDATION-SET OVERFITTING (their inner-CV scores are optimistically biased), which TabArena flags as a model-development problem. (5) Holdout validation (vs nested CV) systematically underestimates all models and biases toward models that already ensemble internally. Metric: Elo (ROC AUC binary, log-loss multiclass, RMSE regression; 1000 Elo = default RandomForest).
baselines_compared: All 16 models compared against each other plus AutoGluon 1.3 (4h) reference pipeline; the paper also systematically compares TabArena's evaluation design against 13 prior tabular benchmarks (Grinsztajn et al., McElfresh et al., Gorishniy et al., Gijsbers/AMLB, Holzmüller et al., Ye et al., Salinas & Erickson, etc.) on inner/outer splits, ensembling, manual curation, datasets remaining, result reuse, and HPO limits.
transferable_idea: For a 70+-heterogeneous-feature tokenizer project, the most reusable thing here is the EVALUATION PROTOCOL, not a model: (a) you cannot claim a new tokenizer beats GBDTs unless you compare under tuned + post-hoc-ensembled, nested-CV conditions — single-config or holdout comparisons systematically understate strong tokenizers and overstate models that ensemble internally; (b) report performance stratified by feature-count and sample-count buckets, because tokenizer advantage is regime-specific (per-cell foundation tokenizers only proven <=500 features / small samples); (c) treat validation-set overfitting as a first-class failure mode — a tokenizer that looks great in inner-CV but is overrepresented in ensembles may be memorizing the validation fold. TabArena's 51-dataset OpenML suite (ID 457) and precomputed result artifacts are a ready, reusable testbed for the static-numerical+categorical part of this topic.
limitations: Author-stated v0.1 scope limits: IID classification/regression only; small-to-medium data (500-250k samples); NO temporal/non-IID/time-series, NO very-small (<500) or large (>250k) data, no clustering/anomaly/survival. Only 51 datasets survived curation from 1053 investigated — small absolute count and curation involves subjective human judgment (criteria applied lazily: first exclusion reason wins, not exhaustive). Foundation-model comparisons are confined to <=500-feature subsets, so no verdict on wide-table foundation tokenizers. Compute cost is enormous (~15 wall-clock-years) and Elo neglects effect-size magnitude.
pitfalls: 1) This is a benchmark, NOT a tokenization method — do not cite it as a technique; cite it as the fair-evaluation context that bounds claims about FT-Transformer-lineage and foundation-model tokenizers. 2) The headline 'deep learning has caught up' is conditional on tuning + post-hoc ensembling AND on the small-to-medium IID regime; under tight compute or single-config, CatBoost/GBDTs still lead — do not over-read the leaderboard. 3) Foundation-model wins (TabPFNv2) are ONLY on <=500-feature / small-sample subsets; this is directly relevant because this topic targets 70+ (possibly hundreds of) features at larger scale where TabArena provides NO foundation-model evidence. 4) Temporal data is excluded by design, so TabArena says nothing about the temporal-static-fusion layer central to this topic. 5) Validation-set overfitting inflating deep models in ensembles is a reproducibility warning: a tokenizer tuned to inner-CV may not transfer — verify on held-out outer folds. 6) Elo is calibrated to default RandomForest = 1000 and ignores magnitude, so small but practically meaningful gaps can be invisible.
relevance_to_topic: It is the authoritative fair-comparison harness that tells you WHEN the deep/foundation-model tokenizers in this topic (per-feature embeddings in RealMLP/TabM, per-cell prior-fitted tokens in TabPFNv2/TabICL/TabDPT) actually beat GBDTs on real heterogeneous numerical+categorical tables — and it shows that proof exists only for the <=500-feature, small-to-medium, IID regime, leaving the 70+-feature-at-scale and temporal parts of the topic unvalidated.
code_available: yes — https://tabarena.ai ; code, leaderboard, precomputed results, and OpenML suite 'tabarena-v0.1' (suite ID 457). Models implemented atop AutoGluon AbstractModel; reproducible pipelines and contribution protocols published.
